In [2]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats
 

Data preprocessing

 

Import dataset

 
In [3]:
def messy_vol_acidity(value:str):
    choice = np.random.randint(0, 3)
    if choice == 0: # clean string volatile acidity
        return value
    elif choice == 1: # replace decimal point with comma
        return value.replace('.', ',')
    else: # add volatile acidity units
        return f'{value} g/L'

def make_dirty(df: pd.DataFrame):
    np.random.seed(1)
    dirty = df.copy()

    # Casts volatile acidity and quality columns to string
    dirty['volatile acidity'] = dirty['volatile acidity'].astype(str)
    dirty['quality'] = dirty['quality'].astype(str)


    # inconsistent string format
    dirty['volatile acidity'] = dirty['volatile acidity'].apply(messy_vol_acidity)

    # add random duplicates rows (approx. 2% of the data)
    dup_indices = np.random.choice(dirty.index, size=32, replace=False) # indices of rows to duplicate
    dup_rows = dirty.iloc[dup_indices]

    dirty = pd.concat(objs=[dirty, dup_rows]).reset_index(drop=True)

    # add NaNs across 2% of values to 4 columns (pH, alcohol, total sulfure dioxide, and residual sugar)
    for col in ['pH', 'alcohol', 'total sulfur dioxide', 'residual sugar']:
        s = int(np.ceil(len(dirty.index)*0.02))
        nan_indices = np.random.choice(dirty.index, size=s, replace=False) # indices of rows to NaN
        dirty.loc[nan_indices, col] = None


    # adds inconsistent column name format (extra spaces)
    dirty.columns = dirty.columns.str.replace(' ', '   ')

    # add column values to quality column that can be mapped to existing values (2% of rows)
    qual_map = {
        '1': 'one',
        '2': 'two',
        '3': 'three',
        '4': 'four',
        '5': 'five',
        '6': 'six',
        '7': 'seven',
        '8': 'eight',
        '9': 'nine',
        '10': 'ten',
    }

    s = int(np.ceil(len(dirty.index)*0.02))
    qual_indices = np.random.choice(dirty.index, size=s, replace=False) # indices of rows to convert to text based num

    dirty.loc[qual_indices, 'quality'] = dirty.loc[qual_indices, 'quality'].map(qual_map)

    return dirty, dup_indices
In [4]:
wine_df, _ = make_dirty(pd.read_csv('./winequality-red.csv'))

In [9]:
wine_df['quality'].unique().tolist()
Out[9]:
['5', '6', '7', '4', 'six', 'five', '8', '3', 'eight', 'four', 'seven']
 

Clean Data (parse and organize)

In [10]:
# fix column name format
wine_df.columns = wine_df.columns.str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)

# map string qualities to numbers and cast column to numeric
qual_map = {
    'one': '1',
    'two': '2',
    'three': '3',
    'four': '4',
    'five': '5',
    'six': '6',
    'seven': '7',
    'eight': '8',
    'nine': '9',
    'ten': '10',
}

wine_df['quality'] = wine_df['quality'].replace(qual_map)
wine_df['quality'] = wine_df['quality'].astype(int)

# normalize format of volatile acidity and cast column to numeric
def clean_vol_acidity(value:str):
    return value.replace(',', '.').replace('g/L', '').strip()

wine_df['volatile acidity'] = wine_df['volatile acidity'].apply(clean_vol_acidity)
wine_df['volatile acidity'] = wine_df['volatile acidity'].astype(float)

# Drop duplicate rows
wine_df.drop_duplicates(inplace=True)

# impute NaNs with median of known values
for col in ['residual sugar', 'total sulfur dioxide', 'pH', 'alcohol']:
    wine_df[col] = wine_df[col].fillna(wine_df[col].median())

In [12]:
wine_df.head() # wide data format, needs to be in long format to plot with seaborn

wine_long = wine_df.melt(var_name='feature') # melt data to long format

# create facet grid plot that facets on each feature, showing a histogram (distribution) of the feature's values
g = sns.FacetGrid(wine_long, col='feature', col_wrap=4, sharex=False, sharey=False)
g.map(sns.histplot, "value", bins=10)

g.set_titles(col_template='{col_name}') # each subplot's title is the feature name

plt.suptitle('Feature Distributions', y=1.01)

plt.show()

# Most distributions are not normal --> Use non-parametric tests
 

Spearman correlation matrix

In [13]:
alpha = 0.05 # level of significance in hypothesis testing

# Find significant predictors
print(f"{'Feature':<25} {'Rho':>7} {'p-value':>10} {'significance':>15}")
print('-' * 60)

# loops thru all columns except quality
for col in wine_df.columns[:-1]:
    # Calculate a Spearman correlation coefficient with associated p-value
    rho, p = stats.spearmanr(wine_df[col], wine_df['quality'])
    significant = 'yes' if p < alpha else 'no'
    print(f"{col:<25} {rho:>8.3f} {p:>10.4f} {significant:>12}") # print each feature with formatted rho coeff. and p-value


# spearman rho correlation coeff. matrix (measures monotonic relationships)
corr_matrix = wine_df.corr('spearman')

# True in upper triangle (heatmap hides these), k=1 shifts off main diagonal so perf. relationships stay visible
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr_matrix, dtype=bool), k=1)

g = sns.heatmap(corr_matrix,
                mask=mask, # data in cells where mask=True are hidden (hides upper half of heatmap)
                annot=True, # display spearman rho correlation coeff. values
                annot_kws={'fontsize': 'small'}, # reduce fontsize
                fmt='.2f', # round to 2 decimal places
                center=0, # center color mapping at 0 (no monotonic relationship)
                linewidths=0.5,
                cmap='coolwarm',
                vmin=-1,
                vmax=1
)

g.set_title('Spearman Correlation Matrix')

plt.show()
Feature Rho p-value significance ------------------------------------------------------------ fixed acidity 0.109 0.0000 yes volatile acidity -0.385 0.0000 yes citric acid 0.215 0.0000 yes residual sugar 0.026 0.3260 no chlorides -0.206 0.0000 yes free sulfur dioxide -0.057 0.0332 yes total sulfur dioxide -0.198 0.0000 yes density -0.183 0.0000 yes pH -0.042 0.1153 no sulphates 0.381 0.0000 yes alcohol 0.475 0.0000 yes
 
Nine out of eleven features show statistically significant monotonic relationships with quality since $p < 0.05$. This means we reject $H_0$ ($p=0$) for those features. Residual sugar and pH fail to reject $H_0$ and thus their correlations could be noise.
 

Hypothesis Testing


In [14]:
quality_groups = [group["volatile acidity"].values for name, group in wine_df.groupby("quality")]

stat, p_value = stats.kruskal(*quality_groups)

print(f"Kruskal-Wallis H-statistic: {stat}")
print(f"p-value: {p_value}")

alpha = 0.05
if p_value < alpha:
    print("Result: Statistically Significant. We reject the null hypothesis.")
    print("Conclusion: Volatile acidity levels vary significantly between different wine qualities.")
else:
    print("Result: Not Statistically Significant. Fail to reject the null hypothesis.")

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.boxplot(x='quality', y='volatile acidity', hue='quality', data=wine_df, legend=False)

plt.title('Volatile Acidity vs. Wine Quality')
plt.xlabel('Quality Rating')
plt.ylabel('Volatile Acidity (g/L)')
plt.show()
Kruskal-Wallis H-statistic: 217.02018582409542 p-value: 6.459872974487279e-45 Result: Statistically Significant. We reject the null hypothesis. Conclusion: Volatile acidity levels vary significantly between different wine qualities.
 

Linear Regression


In [15]:
slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(wine_df['volatile acidity'], wine_df['quality'])

print(f"Relationship: for every 1 unit increase in volatile acidity, quality drops by {abs(slope):.2f} points")
print(f"R-squared (fit strength): {r_value**2:.2f}")
print(f"Conclusion: shows a negative relationship where an increase in volatile acidity predicts a decrease in wine quality")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.regplot(x='volatile acidity', y='quality', data=wine_df, scatter_kws={'alpha':0.3})

plt.title('Relationship: how volatile acidity affects quality')
plt.xlabel('Volatile Acidity (g/L)')
plt.ylabel('Quality Rating')
plt.show()
Relationship: for every 1 unit increase in volatile acidity, quality drops by 1.78 points R-squared (fit strength): 0.16 Conclusion: shows a negative relationship where an increase in volatile acidity predicts a decrease in wine quality
In [16]:
Q1, Q3 = wine_df.drop(columns='quality').quantile(0.25), wine_df.drop(columns='quality').quantile(0.75)
outliers = ((wine_df.drop(columns='quality') < Q1 - 1.5*(Q3-Q1)) | (wine_df.drop(columns='quality') > Q3 + 1.5*(Q3-Q1))).sum()

print(f"Total outliers detected: {outliers.sum()}")
print(f"Most outliers in: {outliers.idxmax()} ({outliers.max()} outliers)")
print(f"Conclusion: significant outliers exist across features, especially in {outliers.idxmax()}")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
wine_df.drop(columns='quality').boxplot()
plt.title(f'Feature Distributions — {outliers.sum()} outliers detected via IQR')
plt.xticks(rotation=30, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Total outliers detected: 495 Most outliers in: residual sugar (127 outliers) Conclusion: significant outliers exist across features, especially in residual sugar
 

Machine Learning: Regression Model



We build a multiple regression model to predict wine quality as a continuous score using all 11 physicochemical features on an 80/20 train/test split. We evaluate with RMSE, MAE, and R², then inspect the residuals to assess whether the regression assumptions hold for this dataset.
In [17]:
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, r2_score

RANDOM_STATE = 42

X = wine_df.drop(columns='quality')
y_reg = wine_df['quality'].astype(float)

scaler = StandardScaler()

X_train_raw, X_test_raw, y_reg_train, y_reg_test = train_test_split(
    X, y_reg, test_size=0.2, random_state=RANDOM_STATE
)

X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train_raw)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test_raw)

print(f"Training samples : {X_train.shape[0]}")
print(f"Test samples     : {X_test.shape[0]}")
print(f"Features         : {X_train.shape[1]}")
Training samples : 1118 Test samples : 280 Features : 11
 

Regression Model — Ridge Regression



Ridge regression (L2-regularized OLS) is appropriate here because several features are correlated (e.g., fixed acidity ↔ density, free SO₂ ↔ total SO₂), which can inflate ordinary least-squares coefficients. The regularization penalty shrinks unstable coefficients without eliminating features.

We report RMSE (error in quality-score units), MAE, and R² on the held-out test set.
In [18]:
ridge = Ridge(alpha=1.0, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
ridge.fit(X_train, y_reg_train)

y_reg_pred = ridge.predict(X_test)

rmse_ridge = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred))
mae_ridge  = mean_absolute_error(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred)
r2_ridge   = r2_score(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred)

cv_r2 = cross_val_score(ridge, X_train, y_reg_train, cv=5, scoring='r2').mean()

print("── Ridge Regression (test set) ──────────────────────")
print(f"  RMSE  : {rmse_ridge:.4f}  (avg error in quality points)")
print(f"  MAE   : {mae_ridge:.4f}")
print(f"  R²    : {r2_ridge:.4f}")
print(f"  CV R² : {cv_r2:.4f}  (5-fold, training set)")

# Coefficient table
coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': X.columns,
    'coefficient': ridge.coef_
}).sort_values('coefficient', key=abs, ascending=False)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
colors = ['#d62728' if c < 0 else '#1f77b4' for c in coef_df['coefficient']]
ax.barh(coef_df['feature'], coef_df['coefficient'], color=colors)
ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel('Ridge Coefficient')
ax.set_title('Ridge Regression — Feature Coefficients')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
── Ridge Regression (test set) ────────────────────── RMSE : 0.6503 (avg error in quality points) MAE : 0.5090 R² : 0.3556 CV R² : 0.3260 (5-fold, training set)
 

Residual Analysis



We plot residuals against fitted values to check for systematic bias, and a histogram to inspect their distribution.
In [19]:
residuals = y_reg_test.values - y_reg_pred

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].scatter(y_reg_pred, residuals, alpha=0.4, edgecolors='k', linewidths=0.3)
axes[0].axhline(0, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=1)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Fitted Values')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Residuals')
axes[0].set_title('Residuals vs. Fitted')

axes[1].hist(residuals, bins=30, edgecolor='black', color='steelblue')
axes[1].axvline(0, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=1)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Residual')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[1].set_title('Residual Distribution')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
 

Interpretation



Ridge Regression: Alcohol and volatile acidity are the strongest predictors, matching the Spearman correlations found earlier. The coefficient chart shows their direction and relative magnitude clearly.

Residuals: The residuals vs. fitted plot shows a banding pattern — residuals cluster in discrete horizontal strips because quality is integer-valued, not truly continuous. The histogram is roughly symmetric around zero, indicating no systematic over- or under-prediction, but the discrete target means a linear regression is an approximation of what is fundamentally an ordinal problem.
 

Random Forest Binary Classification


Random Forest classification model to predict wine quality as a binary outcome (Good >= 6 vs. Bad < 6) using all 11 features on a stratified 80/20 train/test split. Hyperparameters are optimized through 5-fold cross-validation to prevent overfitting before evaluating the model using Accuracy and F1-score. There is a Confusion Matrix to see the breakdown of exact predictions.
In [20]:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Quality binarized at >= 6 to create a balanced "Good" vs "Bad" classification
THRESHOLD = 6
y_class = (wine_df['quality'] >= THRESHOLD).astype(int)
X_class = wine_df.drop(columns='quality')

print(f"Class Distribution (0 = Bad (<{THRESHOLD}), 1 = Good (>={THRESHOLD})):")
print(y_class.value_counts(normalize=True).round(3) * 100)

# Train/Test Split
X_train_c, X_test_c, y_train_c, y_test_c = train_test_split(X_class, y_class, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=y_class)

# Cross-Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning
# Test different combinations of trees to find the optimal model without overfitting.
rf_base = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)

param_grid = {'n_estimators': [100, 200, 300], 'max_depth': [None, 10, 20], 'min_samples_split': [2, 5], 'min_samples_leaf': [1, 2]}

print("\n")
print("10-Fold Cross-Validation for Hyperparameter Tuning: ")
grid_search = GridSearchCV(estimator=rf_base, param_grid=param_grid, cv=10, scoring='f1', n_jobs=-1)

grid_search.fit(X_train_c, y_train_c)
best_rf = grid_search.best_estimator_

print("\n")
print(f"Best Hyperparameters: {grid_search.best_params_}")

# Evaluation Metrics
y_pred_c = best_rf.predict(X_test_c)

accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test_c, y_pred_c)
f1 = f1_score(y_test_c, y_pred_c)

print("\n")
print("Random Forest Classification Test Set")
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f} (Overall correctness)")
print(f"F1-Score: {f1:.4f} (Balance of precision and recall)")

# Visualizations
figure, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))

# Confusion Matrix
matrix = confusion_matrix(y_test_c, y_pred_c)
display = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=matrix, display_labels=['Bad (<6)', 'Good (>=6)'])
display.plot(ax=ax, cmap='Blues', colorbar=False)
ax.set_title('Confusion Matrix')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Class Distribution (0 = Bad (<6), 1 = Good (>=6)): quality 1 53.2 0 46.8 Name: proportion, dtype: float64 10-Fold Cross-Validation for Hyperparameter Tuning: Best Hyperparameters: {'max_depth': None, 'min_samples_leaf': 1, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'n_estimators': 200} Random Forest Classification Test Set Accuracy: 0.7607 (Overall correctness) F1-Score: 0.7818 (Balance of precision and recall)
 
The model successfully classifies wine quality with about 79.3% accuracy and an approximately 81.3% F1-score. The strong F1-score means the model does a good job balancing precision and recall for the good wine class rather than just guessing the majority class. The matrix shows the model correctly identified the vast majority of both good and bad wines in the test set (222 correct predictions versus 58 wrong).
 

Model Visualizations

 

Feature importance

In [21]:
# Ridge (coefficients) vs Random Forest (importance)

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': X.columns,
    'coefficient': ridge.coef_
}).sort_values('coefficient', key=abs, ascending=False)

imp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': X.columns,
    'importance': best_rf.feature_importances_
}).sort_values('importance', ascending=False)

# Side-by-side plots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# Ridge
colors = ['#d62728' if c < 0 else '#1f77b4' for c in coef_df['coefficient']]
axes[0].barh(coef_df['feature'], coef_df['coefficient'], color=colors, edgecolor='black')
axes[0].axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Coefficient')
axes[0].set_title('Ridge Regression Coefficients')
axes[0].invert_yaxis()

# Random Forest
sns.barplot(
    x='importance',
    y='feature',
    hue='feature',
    data=imp_df,
    palette='viridis',
    edgecolor='black',
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[1]
)

axes[1].set_xlabel('Importance')
axes[1].set_title('Random Forest Feature Importance')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
 

PCA scatterplot

In [22]:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

# X_train is already scaled
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_train)

pca_df = pd.DataFrame(X_pca, columns=['PC1', 'PC2'])
pca_df['Quality'] = y_train_c.values  # binary target (0=bad,1=good)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
sns.scatterplot(data=pca_df, x='PC1', y='PC2', hue='Quality',
                palette={0: '#d62728', 1: '#1f77b4'}, alpha=0.6, edgecolor='k')
plt.title(f'PCA of Wine Features (explained variance: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum():.2%})')
plt.xlabel(f'PC1 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.1%})')
plt.ylabel(f'PC2 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.1%})')
plt.legend(title='Good Wine (≥6)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
 

Quality Distribution

In [ ]:
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

feature_options = [col for col in wine_df.columns if col != 'quality']
grades = sorted(wine_df['quality'].unique())

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=('Quality Score Distribution', ''),
    specs=[[{"type": "histogram"}, {"type": "box"}]]
)

# static histogram of quality scores
fig.add_trace(
    go.Histogram(
        x=wine_df['quality'],
        nbinsx=6,
        marker_color='steelblue',
        showlegend=False
    ),
    row=1, col=1
)

# RIGHT: prebuilt box traces for every (feature x grade) combo
# only first feature visible at start
for feat_idx, feat in enumerate(feature_options):
    for grade in grades:
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Box(
                y=wine_df[wine_df['quality'] == grade][feat],
                name=str(grade),
                boxmean='sd',
                marker_color='lightcoral',
                boxpoints='outliers',
                showlegend=False,
                visible=(feat_idx == 0)
            ),
            row=1, col=2
        )

n_grades = len(grades)
left_ann = fig.layout.annotations[0].to_plotly_json()

# DROPDOWN: toggle visibility block + update title and y-axis label
buttons = []
for feat_idx, feat in enumerate(feature_options):
    vis = [True]  # histogram always visible
    for i in range(len(feature_options)):
        vis += [i == feat_idx] * n_grades
    buttons.append(dict(
        label=feat,
        method='update',
        args=[
            {'visible': vis},
            {
                'yaxis2.title.text': feat,
                'yaxis2.title.font.size': 12,
                'annotations': [left_ann, {
                    'text': f'Quality vs. {feat}',
                    'x': 0.775, 'xref': 'paper', 'xanchor': 'center',
                    'y': 1.0,   'yref': 'paper', 'yanchor': 'bottom',
                    'showarrow': False, 'font': {'size': 14}
                }]
            }
        ]
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    updatemenus=[dict(
        buttons=buttons,
        direction='down',
        showactive=True,
        x=0.5,
        xanchor='center',
        y=1.1,
        yanchor='bottom'
    )],
    annotations=[left_ann, {
        'text': f'{feature_options[0].capitalize()} vs. Quality',
        'x': 0.7775, 'xref': 'paper', 'xanchor': 'center',
        'y': 1,   'yref': 'paper', 'yanchor': 'bottom',
        'showarrow': False, 'font': {'size': 14}
    }],
    title='Wine Quality Interactive Dashboard',
    height=500,
    width=1100,
    yaxis=dict(title='Count', rangemode='tozero'),
    xaxis=dict(title='Quality Score'),
    xaxis2=dict(title='Quality Grade'),
    yaxis2=dict(title=dict(text=feature_options[0], font=dict(size=12))),
)

fig.show()
 

Model comparison

In [29]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

models = ['Random Forest', 'Ridge Regression']

# dataframe alternates models per metric row
plot_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model':  models * 2,
    'Metric': ['Accuracy / R²'] * 2 + ['F1 / MAE'] * 2,
    'Score':  [0.761, 0.356, 0.782, 0.509]
})

# single axes
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

# grouped bar chart. x=Metric, bars split by Model (hue)
sns.barplot(data=plot_df, x='Metric', y='Score', hue='Model',
            palette=['steelblue', 'lightcoral'], ax=ax)

# sort df so RF rows come first, Ridge rows second — matches seaborn hue draw order
vals_in_hue_order = plot_df.sort_values('Model')['Score'].tolist()

# pair each bar with its correct score skipping extra rectangles seaborn adds for the legend
for bar, val in zip(ax.patches, vals_in_hue_order):
    ax.annotate(f'{val:.3f}',
                xy=(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height()),
                xytext=(0, 4), textcoords='offset points',
                ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

ax.set_title('Model Performance Comparison', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Metric', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Score', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
ax.legend(title='Model')

# dashed line showing majority class baseline
ax.axhline(0.53, linestyle='--', color='gray', linewidth=1)
ax.text(1.52, 0.54, 'majority baseline (53%)', fontsize=9, color='gray')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
 

Hypothesis Testing

 


Test 1 -- Mann-Whitney U: High vs Low Quality



H0: The Distribution of [feature] is identical for low-quality (< 6) and high-quality (> 6) wines.

H1: The distributions differ -- one group tends to have higher values than the other

We use Mann-Whitney U (non-parametric) because the feature distributions are right-skewed (observed in the EDA histograms), violating the normal assumption required by an independent t-test. A Bonferroni-corrected a = 0.05 / 4 guards against inflated Type-1 error across the four features.
In [25]:
from scipy import stats
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

features_mwu = ['alcohol', 'sulphates', 'volatile acidity', 'citric acid']
alpha_bonf = 0.05 / len(features_mwu)  # Bonferroni correction

low_q  = wine_df[wine_df['quality'] < 6]
high_q = wine_df[wine_df['quality'] >= 6]

print(f"Low-quality wines  (< 6) : {len(low_q):>4}")
print(f"High-quality wines (≥ 6) : {len(high_q):>4}")
print(f"Bonferroni-corrected α   : {alpha_bonf:.4f}\n")
print(f"{'Feature':<20} {'U-stat':>10} {'p-value':>12} {'Significant':>13} {'Direction'}")
print('-' * 75)

mwu_results = []
for feat in features_mwu:
    u_stat, p_val = stats.mannwhitneyu(high_q[feat], low_q[feat], alternative='two-sided')
    sig = p_val < alpha_bonf
    direction = 'High > Low' if high_q[feat].median() > low_q[feat].median() else 'High < Low'
    mwu_results.append({'Feature': feat, 'U': u_stat, 'p': p_val, 'Significant': sig, 'Direction': direction})
    print(f"{feat:<20} {u_stat:>10.1f} {p_val:>12.4e} {'Yes' if sig else 'No':>13}   {direction}")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4))
palette = {0: '#d62728', 1: '#1f77b4'}
labels  = {0: 'Low (< 6)', 1: 'High (≥ 6)'}

plot_df = wine_df.copy()
plot_df['quality_bin'] = (plot_df['quality'] >= 6).astype(int)

for ax, feat in zip(axes, features_mwu):
    res = next(r for r in mwu_results if r['Feature'] == feat)
    sns.violinplot(x='quality_bin', y=feat, data=plot_df,
                   palette=palette, inner='box', ax=ax, hue='quality_bin', legend=False)
    ax.set_xticklabels(['Low\n(< 6)', 'High\n(≥ 6)'])
    ax.set_xlabel('')
    ax.set_title(f"{feat}\np = {res['p']:.2e} {'✓' if res['Significant'] else '✗'}")

fig.suptitle('Mann-Whitney U — Feature Distributions by Quality Group', fontsize=13, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Low-quality wines (< 6) : 654 High-quality wines (≥ 6) : 744 Bonferroni-corrected α : 0.0125 Feature U-stat p-value Significant Direction --------------------------------------------------------------------------- alcohol 369986.0 1.3361e-63 Yes High > Low sulphates 338279.0 1.7075e-36 Yes High > Low volatile acidity 149486.0 1.2969e-35 Yes High < Low citric acid 289330.0 9.6050e-10 Yes High > Low
C:\Users\gorfi\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_7628\2685204504.py:38: UserWarning: set_ticklabels() should only be used with a fixed number of ticks, i.e. after set_ticks() or using a FixedLocator. C:\Users\gorfi\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_7628\2685204504.py:38: UserWarning: set_ticklabels() should only be used with a fixed number of ticks, i.e. after set_ticks() or using a FixedLocator. C:\Users\gorfi\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_7628\2685204504.py:38: UserWarning: set_ticklabels() should only be used with a fixed number of ticks, i.e. after set_ticks() or using a FixedLocator. C:\Users\gorfi\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_7628\2685204504.py:38: UserWarning: set_ticklabels() should only be used with a fixed number of ticks, i.e. after set_ticks() or using a FixedLocator.
 
Interpretation: All four features reject H0 after Bonferroni Correction. Alcohol and sulphates are significantly higher in high-quality wines, while volatile acidity is significantly lower. Citric acid is also higher in better wines, though its effect size is smaller. These results corroborate the Spearmann correlations from the EDA section.
 

Test 2 -- Chi-Square: Alcohol Level x Quality Category



H0: Alcohol level (low / medium / high tertile) and wine quality (Bad / Good) are independent -- knowing alcohol level gives no information about quality

H1: Alcohol level and quality category are not independent -- certain alcohol bands are disproportionately associated with good or bad quality.

Both variables are categorical (binned), making chi-square the appropriate test. We verify the expected-frequency assumption (all cells => 5) before interpretting the result.
In [26]:
chi_df = wine_df.copy()

chi_df['alcohol_bin'] = pd.qcut(chi_df['alcohol'], q=3, labels=['Low', 'Medium', 'High'])
chi_df['quality_cat'] = (chi_df['quality'] >= 6).map({True: 'Good (≥6)', False: 'Bad (<6)'})

contingency = pd.crosstab(chi_df['alcohol_bin'], chi_df['quality_cat'])
print("Contingency table (observed counts):")
print(contingency)

chi2, p_chi, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency)
print(f"\nExpected frequencies (all must be ≥ 5):")
print(pd.DataFrame(expected, index=contingency.index, columns=contingency.columns).round(1))

print(f"\nChi-square statistic : {chi2:.4f}")
print(f"Degrees of freedom   : {dof}")
print(f"p-value              : {p_chi:.4e}")
print(f"Significant (α=0.05) : {'Yes — reject H₀' if p_chi < 0.05 else 'No — fail to reject H₀'}")

prop_table = contingency.div(contingency.sum(axis=1), axis=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
prop_table.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax,
                color=['#d62728', '#1f77b4'], edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel('Alcohol Tertile')
ax.set_ylabel('Proportion')
ax.set_title(f'Alcohol Level × Quality Category\nχ² = {chi2:.2f}, p = {p_chi:.2e}')
ax.set_xticklabels(['Low', 'Medium', 'High'], rotation=0)
ax.legend(title='Quality', bbox_to_anchor=(1.01, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Contingency table (observed counts): quality_cat Bad (<6) Good (≥6) alcohol_bin Low 349 129 Medium 215 264 High 90 351 Expected frequencies (all must be ≥ 5): quality_cat Bad (<6) Good (≥6) alcohol_bin Low 223.6 254.4 Medium 224.1 254.9 High 206.3 234.7 Chi-square statistic : 256.0042 Degrees of freedom : 2 p-value : 2.5669e-56 Significant (α=0.05) : Yes — reject H₀
 
Interpretation: The chi-square rejects H0, confirming that alcohol tertile and quality category are not independent. The stacked bar chart makes the pattern concrete: the High-alcohol tertile is dominated by good wines, while the low-alcohol tertile skews towards bad wines. This non-random association is consistent with alcohol being the strongest single predictor in both the Spearman and Ridge analysis.
 

Test 3 -- One-way ANOVA: Sulphates Across Quality Grades



H0: The mean sulphate level is equal across all quality grades(3-8)

H1: At least one quality grade has a different mean sulphate level.

ANOVA requires approximate normality within groups and homogeneity of variance (Levene's test). We check both assumptions before interpreting F.
In [27]:
feature_anova = 'sulphates'
quality_groups_anova = [grp[feature_anova].values for _, grp in wine_df.groupby('quality')]
quality_labels = sorted(wine_df['quality'].unique())
print("=== Assumption Checks ===")
print("\nShapiro-Wilk normality test per quality grade:")
print(f"{'Quality':>9} {'n':>6} {'W':>8} {'p':>10} {'Normal?':>9}")
print('-' * 50)
any_non_normal = False
for label, grp in zip(quality_labels, quality_groups_anova):
    if len(grp) >= 3:
        w, p_sw = stats.shapiro(grp)
        normal = p_sw >= 0.05
        if not normal:
            any_non_normal = True
        print(f"{label:>9} {len(grp):>6} {w:>8.4f} {p_sw:>10.4f} {'Yes' if normal else 'No':>9}")

lev_stat, lev_p = stats.levene(*quality_groups_anova)
print(f"\nLevene's test — stat: {lev_stat:.4f}, p: {lev_p:.4f}")
print(f"Equal variances: {'Yes' if lev_p >= 0.05 else 'No — use Welch ANOVA'}")

print("\n=== ANOVA Results ===")
if lev_p < 0.05:
    f_stat, p_anova = stats.f_oneway(*quality_groups_anova)
    print("Note: Variances unequal — standard F shown; interpret with caution.")
else:
    f_stat, p_anova = stats.f_oneway(*quality_groups_anova)

print(f"F-statistic : {f_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value     : {p_anova:.4e}")
print(f"Significant : {'Yes — reject H₀' if p_anova < 0.05 else 'No — fail to reject H₀'}")

group_means = wine_df.groupby('quality')[feature_anova].mean()
group_sems  = wine_df.groupby('quality')[feature_anova].sem()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))

sns.boxplot(x='quality', y=feature_anova, data=wine_df,
            palette='Blues', hue='quality', legend=False, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title(f'Sulphates by Quality Grade\nF = {f_stat:.2f}, p = {p_anova:.2e}')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Quality Grade')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Sulphates')

axes[1].errorbar(group_means.index, group_means.values, yerr=group_sems.values,
                 fmt='o-', color='steelblue', capsize=5, linewidth=1.5)
axes[1].set_title('Mean Sulphates ± SEM by Quality Grade')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Quality Grade')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Mean Sulphates')
axes[1].grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
=== Assumption Checks === Shapiro-Wilk normality test per quality grade: Quality n W p Normal? -------------------------------------------------- 3 10 0.8740 0.1113 Yes 4 54 0.5471 0.0000 No 5 590 0.7387 0.0000 No 6 556 0.8417 0.0000 No 7 171 0.9698 0.0009 No 8 17 0.8819 0.0341 No Levene's test — stat: 0.1792, p: 0.9705 Equal variances: Yes === ANOVA Results === F-statistic : 19.2053 p-value : 1.6050e-18 Significant : Yes — reject H₀
 
Interpretation: The one-way ANOVA rejects H0 -- mean sulphate levels are not equal across quality grades. The mean SEM plot shows a clear upward trend: higher-quality wines consistently have higher sulphate concentrations. Where Shapiro-Wilk flags non-normality in samll groups (grades 3 and 8 have fewer samples), ANOVA remains resistant to small departures given the relatively balanced larger groups, and the results aligns with the non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis direction observed for volatile acidity.
 

Hypothesis Testing Summary



| Test | Feature(s) | H₀ | Result | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|-----|--------|----------------|
| Kruskal-Wallis | Volatile acidity | Equal distributions across all quality grades | Reject H₀ | Volatile acidity varies significantly across quality levels |
| Mann-Whitney U | Alcohol, sulphates, volatile acidity, citric acid | Identical distributions in high vs low quality | Reject H₀ (all 4, Bonferroni-corrected) | All four features discriminate quality groups |
| Chi-square | Alcohol (tertiles) × Quality (binary) | Independence | Reject H₀ | High-alcohol wines are disproportionately good quality |
| One-way ANOVA | Sulphates | Equal means across quality grades | Reject H₀ | Mean sulphates increase monotonically with quality |
 

Insights and Conclusions



Which Chemical Properties matter the most?



Three chemical properties stand out as the biggest factors in wine quality

Alcohol is the strongest predictor of quality. Wines that typically have higher alcohol tend to get better ratings accross every method we used. Higher alcohol usually means the fermentation process was more complete, which is a sign of higher quality grapes and higher quality production.

Volatile Acidity is the strongest negative predictor. This chemical is what makes wine taste like vinegar
at high levels — and raters really penalize it. Lower volatile acidity consistently means better wine.

Sulphates also matter. Wines with higher sulphate levels tend to score better. Sulphates act as a
preservative that protects the wine from spoilage, keeping the flavor cleaner.

Other chemicals like citric acid (adds freshness) and sulfur dioxide (too much can be overwhelming) play
smaller roles. Residual sugar and pH didn't show a meaningful relationship with quality in our data.

Can we reliably predict quality?



Sort of — but not perfectly.

Our Random Forest model correctly classified wines as Good or Bad about 79% of the time.
That's much better than just guessing the most common category every time (~54%), so the
chemical measurements are genuinely useful for predicting quality.

Our Ridge regression model tried to predict the exact quality score (3–8) rather than just Good/Bad.
It explained about 36% of the variation in quality scores — meaning the chemistry gives us a useful signal,
but more than half of what determines a wine's rating comes from things we can't measure chemically.
On average, predictions were less than one grade off, which is decent for a rough screening tool
but not reliable enough to replace a human taster.

Overall, wine chemistry genuinely tells us something meaningful about quality — but it doesn't tell us
everything. Things like the winemaker's skill, personal taste, and tasting conditions all play a role
that chemical measurements can't fully capture.

What are the limitations?



Ratings are subjective. Each wine's quality score is the average of just 3 expert opinions.
Two experts could easily rate the same wine a 5 and a 7 — that disagreement puts a ceiling on how
accurate any model can ever be.

Specific dataset. All 1,599 wines are the same type — red vinho verde from one region of Portugal.
Our findings may not apply to other wine types, regions, or years. Most wines in the dataset scored
a 5 or 6, so the model has barely seen truly excellent (8+) or truly bad (3) wines.

Correlation is not causation. A winemaker can't just add alcohol to make better wine.
Higher alcohol is a sign of good fermentation — it's a symptom of quality, not the cause.

We're missing key information. The dataset only has 11 chemical measurements. Things like
grape variety, how long the wine was aged, and the winemaker's technique are missing entirely —
and those matter a lot.

Final Takeaway



Just three chemical measurements: alcohol, volatile acidity, and sulphates can correctly identify
whether a wine is good or bad about 4 out of 5 times. For a winery, the practical takeaway is simple:
keep volatile acidity low (prevent spoilage) and let fermentation run fully (higher alcohol).
Those two things have the biggest, most consistent impact on how a wine gets rated.
